commits.message:
mit-story-5rm.1: voice pass — one narrator from masthead to epilogue

Read every reader-facing line in OPENING/NEST/TREE (plus the painter-baked
captions and the epilogue) in scroll order and settled the register: a docent
in third-person-present describing the live mechanism, turning to second person
only to credit the reader's scroll. Code voice (typed CSS, API names),
instrument/citation labels, and the foundry sub-voice in the forms gallery are
intentional and kept distinct.

Rewrote the outliers that broke the one-narrator contract:
- unified the 'no-code' refrain spelling in narrator prose: bind caption
  'No JS.' -> 'No JavaScript.' (JS now only in code voice + the terse bar title)
- standardised reader-copy spelling to British: 'viewport center' -> 'centre'
  (to match 'metre by metre')
- horizontal-pin panel titles were 3 imperatives + 1 noun; made them all
  third-person-present description: 'The stage pins / It moves sideways /
  The pin releases / Same idea'
- smoothed clipped 'onStep marks that step the active one.' ->
  '...as active.'

[FRAMING:representation] the copy IS the page's voice; an incoherent register
is a representation that drifts from the page. Verified live in Chrome: edits
render, no console errors, no caption overflow at mobile width.

mit-styling-aj1.10: the epilogue — an active outro that dissolves its closing words into the paper (#44)

* mit-styling-aj1.10: the epilogue — an active outro that lifts its closing words off the page and dissolves them into the paper

The Epilogue was a still light bookend to the hero; now it performs its own exit.
The tall section is pure scroll runway and the deck pins (a descent-frame's sticky,
but in the light register) holding the closing words readable. Keep scrolling and
the lines leave on staggered windows — the rule collapses to its own centre, the
supporting marks lift away first, the masthead 'That's scrollytelling.' last and
slowest, widening its letter-spacing as it bleaches into the paper (the sentence
letting go), the back-to-top link the final mark — until the frame is empty
bone-white page: the piece ends on the blank sheet it began on.

The exit is a pure function of scroll progress p, so it is reduced-motion-safe by
construction (only the opacity dissolve survives the mo gate; all movement drops).
The outro is its own part — a build (refs + exit windows) + paint(p) seam — composed
into the single scroll owner by ONE uniform call, never special-cased inside the
shared node loop. No locked idiom duplicated: luminance + vertical drift, not
aberration / bokeh / HUD / chart / iris.

Verified in real Chrome across the full scroll range (rest -> staggered exit ->
empty page); zero console errors from the page.

* epilogue outro: measure progress baseline off the stable container, not the pinned stage

The outro's cached start position was read from .epi-stage's bounding rect. While
that stage is sticky-pinned, its rect-top is the viewport-pinned position (~0), so a
resize or font reflow mid-outro rebased the baseline to the current scroll and snapped
the exit animation back to the start — an ambient temporal coupling on incidental
sticky state at measure time. Measure top off the never-sticky .epilogue container
instead: it sits at the same natural top the stage pins to but never moves, so layout
changes can't shift the baseline. Drop the now-unused stage ref.

Verified in real Chrome: with the stage pinned (rect-top 0), a resize event mid-outro
leaves the masthead opacity unchanged (no snap-back).
